title: Non-Bloch band theory of boundary-controlled magnon edge modes in an antiferromagnetic chain
authors: Suman Debnath, Sonu Verma, Rohit Mukherjee, Arijit Kundu
abstract: We define a winding number within the Non-Bloch band theory framework that captures the emergence of magnon edge modes in a one-dimensional antiferromagnetic spin chain, even when the conventional Bloch winding number is trivial. Within linear spin-wave theory, magnon excitations are governed by a non-Hermitian dynamic matrix, despite the underlying Hamiltonian being Hermitian. The symmetry classification of this matrix yields a trivial bulk invariant, however, finite systems exhibit boundary-localized modes, signaling a breakdown of the conventional bulk-boundary correspondence. We further show that these edge modes can be controlled via boundary perturbations. By tuning the boundary potential, the modes can be driven into or out of the bulk spectrum. To resolve the bulk-boundary mismatch, we develop a non-Bloch framework based on a generalized Brillouin zone and a winding number that correctly predicts the presence of edge states. Our results establish boundary-controlled topological transitions that are experimentally accessible through local Zeeman fields or modified edge anisotropy in antiferromagnetic van der Waals nanostructures.
summary: The paper develops a non-Bloch topological framework to analyze magnon edge modes in 1D antiferromagnetic chains. It resolves the conflict between trivial bulk invariants and observable boundary states by defining a generalized winding number. This method successfully predicts that these edge modes are controllable via local perturbations, suggesting experimental realization using engineered magnetic nanostructures.
main_problem: The study addresses the emergence of magnon edge modes in an antiferromagnetic spin chain when conventional Bloch topological invariants fail to predict their existence, indicating a breakdown of bulk-boundary correspondence.
main_result: A non-Bloch band theory utilizing a generalized Brillouin zone and a specialized winding number successfully predicts the presence and tunability of these boundary-localized magnon edge states.
method: The authors employ linear spin-wave theory, constructing a pseudo-Hermitian dynamic matrix. They then develop a non-Bloch framework to calculate topological invariants by solving finite-difference equations on a generalized Brillouin zone.
model_or_system: The system is a one-dimensional antiferromagnetic spin chain described by a Hamiltonian including Heisenberg exchange, Dzyaloshinskii–Moriya interaction (DMI), and easy-axis anisotropy. The analysis considers open boundary conditions (OBC) and tunable boundary perturbations.
key_observables: Magnon excitation spectrum eigenvalues, non-Bloch winding number ($W_{nB}$), Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), and the energy levels of edge states as a function of boundary potential ($\epsilon$).
important_parameters: DMI strength ($D$), anisotropy ($\kappa$), exchange couplings ($J_1, J_2$), and the magnitude of the boundary perturbation ($\epsilon$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on linear spin-wave theory. For detailed winding number calculations, the DMI term is often set to zero ($D=0$) for analytical tractability.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the antiferromagnetic chain structure, energy spectra showing edge states appearing/disappearing with $\epsilon$, and plots of the non-Bloch winding number across the generalized Brillouin zone.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the failure of Bloch theory to defining the non-Bloch framework. It then applies this theory to calculate topological invariants, demonstrating how these invariants correctly track the appearance and disappearance of edge states controlled by boundary perturbations.
why_interesting: This work provides a rigorous theoretical tool (non-Bloch topology) to characterize localized excitations in magnetic systems where standard bulk band theory fails, offering insights applicable to engineered quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic diagram of the antiferromagnetic chain. The spins interact with nearest neighbors by Heisenberg exchange interactions J1, J2. The spins interact to next nearest neighbour by DMI. The sign of DMI (νij = ±1) changes alternatively as shown. Each spin has an easy-axis anisotropy energy κ (not shown) with the easy-axis directed along z−direction. The up and down spins are denoted by A and B, respectively.

FIG. 2. Magnon dispersion for different parameter values. (a) For D = 0, κ = 0, and J1 = J2 = 1, the system reduces to the Heisenberg model, exhibiting two degenerate gapless Goldstone modes. It is shown as overlapping blue solid and red dashed curves in the figure. Here, a0 = 2d is the lattice constant. (b) Introducing finite anisotropy (κ = 0.05) opens a gap at zero energy, with magnitude 2 √

FIG. 3. The energy spectrum and wavefunction profiles for a finite system under open boundary conditions. (a) Energy spectrum as a function of J1/J2, with D = 0 and κ = 0.05 fixed. The color scale represents the inverse participation ratio (IPR). Edge modes are present throughout the parameter range shown. (b) Energy spectrum as a function of D, with J1/J2 = 1 fixed. The edge states persist for finite D, with only a shift in the overall energy scale. For sufficiently large D, the edge states merge into the bulk continuum and the spectrum develops complex eigenvalues, signaling the onset of instability; this occurs around Df c ≈0.63. Beyond this point, the spectrum is plotted in black. (c)...

FIG. 4. Energy spectrum, winding number, and generalized Brillouin zone (GBZ). (a)–(c) Energy as a function of ϵ for J1 = 0.5, 1, 1.5, respectively, with J2 = 1, D = 0, κ = 0.05 fixed. The color scale represents the inverse participation ratio (IPR). In all cases, edge states appear under open boundary conditions (OBC) i.e. at ϵ = 0. As ϵ is increased, these states merge into the bulk spectrum and subsequently re-emerge from the other side of the bands. (d)–(f) Shows the corresponding non-Bloch winding number, capturing the appearance and disappearance of edge modes (also indicated by vertical blue dashed lines).
